paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 07: The Last Lap (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary Four boy running the anchor leg of the 4x100m relay in his last school sports day, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [14 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 10 short-answer items (Q1–Q10) on language use and inference, plus a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q11) on the narrator's emotion arc, are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
It was already half past ten when the boys' relay finals began. Black house had been chasing Yellow all morning, losing in the hurdles by a metre, in the high jump by two centimetres, in the 800m by a single stride. The 4x100m was the last event. Mr Lim, our PE teacher, gathered the four of us under the tentage. 'Two points behind,' he said. 'Just two.'

Paragraph 2
Adam ran the first leg. Lucas ran the second. Daniyal ran the third. Each one of them gave the best run of their life. Adam burned out his calves in the first thirty metres. Lucas held the corner cleanly. Daniyal closed half the gap. But when the baton smacked into my palm, Yellow's anchor was already five metres ahead.

Paragraph 3
The world narrowed to the lane. I could hear the crowd somewhere on my left — students shouting, teachers blowing whistles, my mother somewhere in the bleachers — but the sounds collapsed into one rumbling note behind my heartbeat. I ran. I did not think about my legs; my legs were doing their own thinking.

Paragraph 4
At fifty metres I caught Yellow's anchor. We ran shoulder to shoulder for ten strides — I could see the edge of his cheek through the corner of my eye. He was straining. So was I.

Paragraph 5
At eighty metres he stumbled. Not a fall — just a half-step lost, the kind of stumble you do not even know you have done. But I felt it. I pulled ahead. The white line at the end of the lane came at me very fast.

Paragraph 6
I crossed it 1.2 seconds ahead. I knew the time because the announcer said it twice. The first time, with disbelief; the second time, in celebration.

Paragraph 7
My teammates surrounded me before my own breathing had slowed. Adam threw an arm around my shoulder. Daniyal hugged me from behind. Lucas slapped my back so hard I coughed. Then Mr Lim was there. 'Wei Han,' he said, 'you all did this. Not just you. All four.' Adam nodded so fast he almost dropped his cap.

Paragraph 8
Black won the overall championship by two points. As we walked back to the tentage, I realised something I had not realised while I was running. The crowd had not been cheering for me. They had been cheering for the four of us. My lap had been one of four. And this — the heat in my legs, the dust on my shoes, the four of us walking back together — was the last school sports day I would ever run. I would not have traded any of it for any other event in my life.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. Tentage: the canvas tent at a school sports day under which house members gather between events; CCA: co-curricular activity.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, how were the Black and Yellow houses positioned going into the relay finals? [1]
questions.answer: Black house was two points behind Yellow overall, having lost to Yellow all morning in the hurdles, high jump and 800m by very small margins

questions.stem: Q2. What does 'Adam burned out his calves in the first thirty metres' (Paragraph 2) suggest about how Adam ran his leg? [1]
questions.answer: he gave everything he had right from the start and held nothing back — he pushed himself to his physical limit very early in the race

questions.stem: Q3. What is the effect of the phrase 'the sounds collapsed into one rumbling note behind my heartbeat' (Paragraph 3)? [1]
questions.answer: it conveys how the narrator had tuned out all the external noises — the crowd blurred into a single background sound while his own heartbeat dominated his awareness, showing his deep focus on the run

questions.stem: Q4. In Paragraph 3, what does 'my legs were doing their own thinking' suggest about the narrator's running? [1]
questions.answer: he had stopped consciously controlling his running — his body was running on instinct and training rather than on deliberate moment-by-moment effort

questions.stem: Q5. In Paragraph 5, why does the narrator emphasise that the stumble was 'not a fall — just a half-step lost, the kind of stumble you do not even know you have done'? [1]
questions.answer: to show how close the race was — Yellow's anchor barely faltered, but it was just enough for the narrator to take advantage of and pull ahead

questions.stem: Q6. In Paragraph 6, why did the announcer say the time twice — 'the first time, with disbelief; the second time, in celebration'? [1]
questions.answer: the result was so unexpected that the announcer had to verify it for himself before he could announce it as fact for the crowd to celebrate

questions.stem: Q7. Why did Mr Lim say 'you all did this. Not just you. All four' (Paragraph 7)? [1]
questions.answer: he wanted the narrator to understand that the anchor leg only worked because Adam, Lucas and Daniyal had laid the groundwork — winning was a team accomplishment, not an individual one

questions.stem: Q8. What is the effect of 'Adam nodded so fast he almost dropped his cap' (Paragraph 7)? [1]
questions.answer: it shows Adam's wholehearted, almost comic agreement with Mr Lim — he is so eager for the narrator to hear the message about the team that his enthusiasm overruns his body. It adds a light human touch to a serious moment

questions.stem: Q9. In Paragraph 8, what does the narrator come to realise after the race that he had not realised while running? [1]
questions.answer: that the crowd had been cheering for all four of them, not just for him — his single lap was part of a team effort, and this was his last school sports day

questions.stem: Q10. What is the effect of the closing line 'I would not have traded any of it for any other event in my life'? [1]
questions.answer: it captures the narrator's deep gratitude not just for winning but for the shared experience itself — running with his teammates, the heat, the dust — emphasising that the value of the moment was in the togetherness, not in the medal

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling shifts as the relay unfolds and ends. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes his dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: pressure / focus / euphoria / grateful / anxious / proud / relieved / wistful. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Last Lap' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as the relay unfolds — from pressure as the baton smacks into his palm with Yellow already five metres ahead, to focus as the world narrows and the crowd noise collapses into a single rumbling note, to euphoria as his teammates surround him after the win, ending grateful as he walks back to the tentage realising it was a team effort and his last sports day. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 1-2, with Black two points behind and the baton arriving in his palm five metres behind Yellow's anchor? [1]

Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 3-4, running with the world narrowed to the lane, the crowd noise collapsed into a single note, and his legs doing their own thinking? [1]
Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 6-7, crossing the line 1.2 seconds ahead with his teammates surrounding him before his breathing has slowed? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8, walking back to the tentage and realising the crowd had been cheering for all four of them and this was his last school sports day? [1]
